trajectory_summary: The call opened warm and briefly spiked into genuine engagement when Mike talked about his businesses, but the rep never converted that energy into qualification depth or a booking. The call drifted into personal rapport chat, accepted a logistical obstacle (not home) as a full stop, and ended with a personal-phone follow-up promise instead of an appointment. The outcome is a fragile, unstructured callback with no locked time and no appointment on the calendar.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is not a booked appointment — it is a personal-phone bill-follow-up with no scheduled sit. This should be tagged as 'no_appointment / bill_pending_callback' rather than any booking variant. The call had the raw material for a booking (qualified homeowner, solo title, good credit, first-time solar, pool, Airbnbs, warm tone) and none of it was converted.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Book appointment before bill — separate the two actions
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. What we can do is I can just send you a text from my personal phone, and then just whenever you get home and and have access to one of those, if you could just send me a photo of it, then that'd be great.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep had a solo-title, qualified, warm lead and ended the call with no appointment and a personal-phone follow-up promise. The logistical obstacle ('I'm not home') was real but not a booking blocker — the rep conflated bill collection with appointment scheduling and lost the sit entirely. This is a paycheck-protection issue: no appointment means no sit, no sit means no pay. The rep needs a practiced response to 'I'm not home right now' that books the appointment first and treats the bill as a pre-sit task.

key_moments.quote: Is there anybody else on the title of the home with you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Clean execution. The rep asked the title question directly and got a clear 'No. It would just be me.' This eliminated the spouse-coordination obstacle entirely — no protocol.spouse_decision complication, no co-decider to schedule around. Worth noting because it makes the missed booking that follows more notable: the rep had a solo-decision-maker, qualified lead, and still didn't close an appointment.

key_moments.quote: I think I just clicked on a link or something about it, and then out of nowhere, I started getting calls.
key_moments.stewart_read: This looks like a low-motivation signal but it is actually a curiosity-creation opening. Mike has never looked at solar, has a pool, owns Airbnbs, and is paying ~$300/month on a single-occupant home — meaning his load is likely to grow. The accidental-lead disclosure is the exact moment the schema's interest_in_solar depth probe is designed for: 'What would it mean for you if you could cut that bill down, given everything you've got going on with the properties?' The rep instead validated the annoyance ('That's kinda annoying. Sorry about that.') and moved on.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. A little bit, I guess.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second touch on the accidental-lead pattern. Mike's 'a little bit, I guess' is the lowest-energy interest signal possible. The rep accepted it and moved to utility company. The schema's objection_inversion framework had a move available here: flip the low-interest framing toward the bill ('What you're not interested in is staying with what you have') or anchor to the pool load ('With a pool running, your bill is probably higher in summer — have you ever looked at what that's actually costing you?'). Neither was attempted.

key_moments.quote: No. That's awesome. I actually so I just barely finished real estate school. I've been wanting to get into that kind of stuff.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mike's real estate and Airbnb disclosure at 03:10 was a genuine engagement spike — the highest-energy moment in the call. The rep matched his energy but redirected it entirely toward her own career aspirations rather than back into qualification. The failure here is not setter_scope_discipline (no solar-technical content was discussed) — it is a failure to re-enter the verify sequence after a customer-initiated off-topic pull. The rep had a natural bridge available: 'That's impressive — with multiple properties and Airbnbs, your energy costs must add up. Is the solar interest for this home specifically, or are you thinking about it across the properties?' Instead, the call spent 90 seconds on Utah real estate.
